ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Bhurji; closest_parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03302050) as paneer is a fresh Indian cottage cheese.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Paneer Bhurji; A00LC (Cheese, fresh) is the closest defensible parent category for paneer-based dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Paneer Bhurji or a sufficiently close parent concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Paneer Bhurji is approximately 200 g, typically served as a main dish alongside roti, paratha, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (Cottage Cheese, Indian) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for paneer (cottage cheese) and cooked paneer preparations. Paneer Bhurji is a sautéed preparation of crumbled paneer with onion, tomato, and spices. Values reflect the cooked dish estimated from ICMR-NIN paneer base values adjusted for added oil (~5 g/100 g dish), vegetables (~15% by weight), and spices. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 01016 — Cheese, cottage, creamed) used as secondary cross-reference for protein and fat ranges.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of paneer and paneer-based preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Bhurji as a composite dish. GI estimated at 27 based on the GI of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese), which is a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate dairy food. Cottage cheese GI is reported at approximately 10–30 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The addition of onion, tomato, and spices does not materially raise the GI given the low carbohydrate content of the dish. GL per 200 g serving = (27 × 6.5 × 2) / 100 = 3.5, classified as low.

hypertension.guidance: Paneer Bhurji contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 200 g restaurant serving may contribute 640 mg sodium — roughly 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Bhurji has a low estimated GI (~27) and a low glycemic load (~3.5 per serving), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with low-GI accompaniments such as whole-wheat roti rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Paneer Bhurji provides approximately 6.5 g saturated fat per 100 g primarily from paneer and added oil; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and prefer preparations using minimal oil to keep saturated fat intake within the recommended limit of less than 7% of total energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Bhurji is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish made by crumbling fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese) and sautéing it with onions, tomatoes, green chillies, ginger, garlic, and a blend of spices including cumin, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala, finished with fresh coriander leaves. The dish has a soft, scrambled texture similar to spiced scrambled eggs, with a rich, mildly spiced, tangy flavour profile derived from the tomato-onion masala base. It is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish that is naturally gluten-free and suitable for lacto-vegetarian diets. Paneer Bhurji is commonly served as a main course with Indian flatbreads such as roti, paratha, or naan, or as a filling for wraps and rolls. Nutritionally, it is a good source of calcium (~190 mg/100 g), protein (~10.5 g/100 g), and vitamin B12 (~0.3 mcg/100 g) from the dairy base. Its low glycemic index (~27) and low glycemic load make it appropriate for blood sugar management. The sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. In Singapore, Paneer Bhurji appears on menus at Indian and fusion restaurants, including Turkish-Indian fusion establishments such as Sultan Palace.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Paneer Bhurji?
Is Paneer Bhurji good for diabetics?
What is the GI of Paneer Bhurji?
What does Paneer Bhurji taste like?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer base data and adjusted for the cooked Paneer Bhurji preparation. No single approved-source entry exists for Paneer Bhurji as a composite dish; values are estimated from paneer composition plus cooking additions. GI is an analogous estimate based on cottage cheese GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match was found in a primary approved source.
notes: 1. The input record listed cuisine as 'Turkish' and cuisine_tags as ['Turkish', 'Kebab', 'Shawarma'], which reflects the restaurant context (Sultan Palace Turkish & Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore). However, Paneer Bhurji is an Indian dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian' and region_of_origin to 'India' to reflect the food's true identity. The restaurant context is noted here. 2. The food_name in the input was '141. Panner Bhurji' (with a menu number and a misspelling); the canonical English name 'Paneer Bhurji' has been used. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer or paneer-based dishes. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA SR Legacy cottage cheese data as cross-reference. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Bhurji was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Multilingual names follow the Indian-dish convention: Hindi (Devanagari), Tamil (Tamil script), Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), and Malay are provided. The dish is not Singaporean in origin but is served in Singapore; Indian-origin multilingual naming rules applied.
